source_paper_title: Robust Online Selection with Uncertain Offer Acceptance
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Perez_Salazar_2025, title={Robust Online Selection with Uncertain Offer Acceptance}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0210}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0210}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Perez-Salazar, Sebastian and Singh, Mohit and Toriello, Alejandro}, year={2025}, month=aug, pages={2226–2260} }
context:
Section 9 (Concluding Remarks), page 24, paragraph beginning “We empirically observe …”

We empirically observe that the robust ratio \(\gamma_n^*(p)\) is convex and decreasing as a function of \(p\), and thus we expect the same behavior from \(\gamma_\infty(p)\), though this remains to be proved (see Figure 1).
title: Prove convexity and monotonicity of the optimal robust ratio in acceptance probability
background_latex:
Consider the secretary problem with uncertain acceptance (SP-UA) with a known horizon length \(n\in\mathbb{N}\) and acceptance probability \(p\in(0,1]\). There are \(n\) candidates with distinct absolute ranks \(1\prec 2\prec\cdots\prec n\) (rank \(1\) is best). Candidates arrive in a uniformly random permutation \(\pi=(R_1,\dots,R_n)\) of \([n]=\{1,\dots,n\}\), where \(R_t\) is the absolute rank of the \(t\)-th arrival.

At time \(t\), the decision maker observes only the partial rank \(r_t\in[t]\) of the current candidate among the first \(t\) arrivals. The decision maker chooses either \(\text{offer}\) or \(\text{pass}\). If \(\text{offer}\) is chosen at time \(t\), the current candidate accepts independently with probability \(p\), in which case the process stops and that candidate is selected; if the offer is rejected (probability \(1-p\)), the process continues to \(t+1\). If \(\text{pass}\) is chosen, the process continues to \(t+1\). A policy \(P\) is any (possibly randomized) Markovian rule mapping \((t,r_t)\) to a distribution over \(\{\text{offer},\text{pass}\}\).

For \(k\in[n]\), a \"top \(k\)\" candidate means absolute rank in \([k]\). Define the robust ratio of a policy \(P\) at acceptance probability \(p\) as
\[
\gamma_P(p)=\min_{k\in[n]}\frac{\Pr(\text{policy }P\text{ selects a top-}k\text{ candidate and the offer is accepted})}{\Pr(\text{at least one of the top-}k\text{ candidates would accept an offer})}.
\]
Since each candidate would accept an offer with probability \(p\) independently of everything else, \(\Pr(\text{at least one top-}k\text{ candidate accepts})=1-(1-p)^k\). The optimal robust ratio is
\[
\gamma_n^*(p)=\sup_P\gamma_P(p),
\]
and the asymptotic optimal robust ratio is \(\gamma_\infty^*(p)=\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_n^*(p)\), which exists because \(\gamma_n^*(p)\) is nonincreasing in \(n\) for fixed \(p\).
problem_definition_latex: Establish (or refute) that the optimal robust ratio \(\gamma_n^*(p)\) is a convex and decreasing function of \(p\in(0,1]\) (for fixed \(n\)), and correspondingly that \(\gamma_\infty^*(p)\) is convex and decreasing in \(p\in(0,1]\).
latex:
Consider the secretary problem with uncertain acceptance (SP-UA) with a known horizon length \(n\in\mathbb{N}\) and acceptance probability \(p\in(0,1]\). There are \(n\) candidates with distinct absolute ranks \(1\prec 2\prec\cdots\prec n\) (rank \(1\) is best). Candidates arrive in a uniformly random permutation \(\pi=(R_1,\dots,R_n)\) of \([n]=\{1,\dots,n\}\), where \(R_t\) is the absolute rank of the \(t\)-th arrival.

At time \(t\), the decision maker observes only the partial rank \(r_t\in[t]\) of the current candidate among the first \(t\) arrivals. The decision maker chooses either \(\text{offer}\) or \(\text{pass}\). If \(\text{offer}\) is chosen at time \(t\), the current candidate accepts independently with probability \(p\), in which case the process stops and that candidate is selected; if the offer is rejected (probability \(1-p\)), the process continues to \(t+1\). If \(\text{pass}\) is chosen, the process continues to \(t+1\). A policy \(P\) is any (possibly randomized) Markovian rule mapping \((t,r_t)\) to a distribution over \(\{\text{offer},\text{pass}\}\).

For \(k\in[n]\), a \"top \(k\)\" candidate means absolute rank in \([k]\). Define the robust ratio of a policy \(P\) at acceptance probability \(p\) as
\[
\gamma_P(p)=\min_{k\in[n]}\frac{\Pr(\text{policy }P\text{ selects a top-}k\text{ candidate and the offer is accepted})}{\Pr(\text{at least one of the top-}k\text{ candidates would accept an offer})}.
\]
Since each candidate would accept an offer with probability \(p\) independently of everything else, \(\Pr(\text{at least one top-}k\text{ candidate accepts})=1-(1-p)^k\). The optimal robust ratio is
\[
\gamma_n^*(p)=\sup_P\gamma_P(p),
\]
and the asymptotic optimal robust ratio is \(\gamma_\infty^*(p)=\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_n^*(p)\), which exists because \(\gamma_n^*(p)\) is nonincreasing in \(n\) for fixed \(p\).

Establish (or refute) that the optimal robust ratio \(\gamma_n^*(p)\) is a convex and decreasing function of \(p\in(0,1]\) (for fixed \(n\)), and correspondingly that \(\gamma_\infty^*(p)\) is convex and decreasing in \(p\in(0,1]\).
location_in_paper: Section 9 (Concluding Remarks), page 24, paragraph beginning “We empirically observe …”
open_problem_quote: We empirically observe that the robust ratio \(\gamma_n^*(p)\) is convex and decreasing as a function of \(p\), and thus we expect the same behavior from \(\gamma_\infty(p)\), though this remains to be proved (see Figure 1).
counterexample_suitability_rationale: For fixed finite \(n\), \(\gamma_n^*(p)\) can be computed numerically by solving the paper’s LP for a grid of \(p\) values, so violations of convexity/monotonicity could be searched for computationally. However, proving (or disproving) the property for all \(p\) and all \(n\) (or in the \(n\to\infty\) limit) is not reducible to a finite instance search, and numerical evidence may miss very small or asymptotic violations.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary loop could parameterize policies (e.g., threshold families) and numerically approximate \(\gamma_n^*(p)\) via the LP or simulation to probe the shape in \(p\), potentially discovering candidate extremal behaviors or counterexamples for small \(n\). But establishing a general convexity/monotonicity theorem likely requires analytic arguments beyond code-driven search, and numerical fitness signals only approximate the universal claims.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
No forward-citation evidence is provided beyond the 2024 source paper, so the convexity/monotonicity question remains open as stated. The source paper develops an exact finite-dimensional LP \((LP)_{n,p}\) for \(\gamma_n^*(p)\) and a continuous relaxation \((CLP)_p\) for \(\gamma_\infty^*(p)\), proving monotonicity in the horizon \(n\) but only conjecturing that \(\gamma_n^*(p)\) and \(\gamma_\infty^*(p)\) are decreasing and convex in \(p\). It establishes sharp structure for large \(p\): for \(p\ge p^*\approx 0.594\), \(\gamma_\infty^*(p)=p^{p/(1-p)}\) with an optimal threshold policy that waits until time \(p^{1/(1-p)}\) and then offers to the current best-so-far.

Existing classical SP-UA analyses (Smith 1975; Tamaki 1991) clarify how acceptance uncertainty changes optimal stopping rules, and Smith’s asymptotic \(k=1\) success probability yields the upper bound \(\gamma_\infty^*(p)\le p^{p/(1-p)}\). For small \(p\), the best known universal upper bound comes from prophet-inequality impossibility results (Hill–Kertz 1982; Kertz 1986), giving \(\gamma_\infty^*(p)\le 1/\beta\approx 0.745\); the source paper conjectures this bound is tight as \(p\to 0\). Methodologically, LP/continuous-LP approaches in secretary problems (Buchbinder–Jain–Singh 2014; Chan–Chen–Jiang 2014) and sample-driven stopping (Correa et al. 2024) provide tools that may be adapted to study parameter dependence in \(p\), but no published proof of convexity/monotonicity in \(p\) is currently identified.

sources.title: A secretary problem with uncertain employment
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies SP-UA with objective of selecting the best candidate; derives asymptotic success probability \(p^{1/(1-p)}\) and corresponding optimal policy structure.
sources.connection: Provides the \(k=1\) benchmark and the upper bound \(\gamma_\infty^*(p)\le p^{p/(1-p)}\) used in the source paper; informs how \(\gamma_n^*(p)\) might vary with \(p\).

sources.title: A secretary problem with uncertain employment and best choice of available candidates
sources.theorem_or_result: Analyzes SP-UA under the objective of selecting the best candidate among those who would accept; provides optimal stopping rules (two models of observability).
sources.connection: Contrasts with the robust-ratio objective; helps contextualize how changing \(p\) affects optimal stopping thresholds and may suggest monotonicity/convexity phenomena.

sources.title: Comparisons of stop rule and supremum expectations of iid random variables
sources.theorem_or_result: Establishes worst-case gap between prophet and optimal stopping for i.i.d. sequences; introduces constants leading to \(\beta\approx 1.34\).
sources.connection: Used in the source paper to upper bound \(\gamma_\infty^*(p)\le 1/\beta\); relates robust ratio behavior as \(p\to 0\) and motivates conjectured limit and shape properties.

sources.title: Stop rule and supremum expectations of iid random variables: a complete comparison by conjugate duality
sources.theorem_or_result: Characterizes the optimal prophet-inequality constant \(\beta\) via conjugate duality and an integral equation; yields tight bound \(1/\beta\approx 0.745\).
sources.connection: Provides the constant \(1/\beta\) appearing in the upper bound for \(\gamma_\infty^*(p)\) and informs limiting behavior in \(p\).

sources.title: Secretary problems via linear programming
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces LP formulations for secretary-type problems and derives optimal/approximate policies via primal-dual reasoning.
sources.connection: Methodological precursor to the MDP-to-LP derivation in the source paper; relevant for understanding structural properties that might imply monotonicity/convexity in parameters.

sources.title: Revealing optimal thresholds for generalized secretary problem via continuous lp: impacts on online k-item auction and bipartite k-matching with random arrival order
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops continuous LP limits for generalized secretary problems and extracts optimal threshold structures.
sources.connection: Provides techniques for passing from finite \((LP)_{n,p}\) to continuous relaxations and analyzing parameter dependence, potentially useful for proving convexity/monotonicity in \(p\).

sources.title: Sample-driven optimal stopping: From the secretary problem to the iid prophet inequality
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies secretary/optimal stopping with independent sampling; derives continuous LP characterizations and shows performance limits approaching prophet-inequality constants.
sources.connection: The source paper conjectures \(\lim_{p\to 0}\gamma_\infty^*(p)=1/\beta\) and notes similarity to independent-sampling models; methods may help analyze shape (convexity/monotonicity) in sampling/acceptance parameters.
